raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

You had a dream about winning at Blackjack. Then you woke up. But you still feel great.
You drive your car to a gas station for supplies. Just snacks. Maybe some coffee.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You duck behind a shelf. Slowly, carefully, you crawl toward the back.
The robber is focused on the register. You're almost to the storage room...
You make it. Hide behind boxes of toilet paper and canned goods.
You can hear everything. The shouting. The crying. The crash of the register hitting the floor.
Then sirens. Running footsteps. Silence.
The police find you an hour later, still hiding. Curled up like a child.
Whiskers is already awake, sitting perfectly still on the dashboard, silhouetted against the sunrise. She looks regal.
Having Whiskers by your side makes the world a little less cruel.
Rain on the roof. Not a storm — just rain. The soft kind. The kind that taps a lullaby on the metal above you.
For the first time in weeks, your brain shuts up. No calculations. No strategies. No what-ifs.
Just you, the rain, and eight uninterrupted hours.
You wake up and for three seconds — three beautiful seconds — you forget where you are.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The cards have been waiting for you.

You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
You hear a knock on your window. Cop knock. The heavy, authoritative kind.
You sit up and see a police officer standing outside your car. He's stocky, late 40s,
with a thick mustache and a name tag that reads MARTINEZ.
"License and registration, please."
You fumble around. You don't actually have a registration. You're not even sure where your license is.
He pulls out a small notepad and writes something down.
"I'm not going to ticket you today. But I need you to know — technically, this is a violation. "
"Next time another officer comes by, they might not be as understanding."
He taps the roof of your car twice, gets back in his cruiser, and drives off.
Whiskers knocks your coffee off the dashboard. On purpose. While maintaining eye contact.
The cards in your dreams are always face down. Always.
The dream is simple this time. You're just... not worried. About anything.
No Dealer. No casino. No car. Just a warm room and the knowledge that tomorrow will be okay.
That's the dream. Not a million dollars. Just okay.
You wake up and the okay evaporates. But you remember what it felt like.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Night falls. The game begins.

"Hello, the name's Frank. Now I've got a baseball game to catch, but it looks like you could use some help."
Frank pulls a shiny silver hammer out of his pocket, and walks to the hood of your beaten down wagon.
"My god. This is just awful. Tell you what, I can fix this up for like 75 bucks,
and your engine will be runnin' just as good as mine. You game?"
"What?! How could you not accept my service? I'm the cheapest damn autoshop worker on this here planet!
But NOOOO, NOT FRANK! Never Frank. He Voted For Trump!
Let's all ridicule frank for his political party. You god damn liberals."
Frank spits in your face, and get back in his truck.
You watch as he revs his engine, gets out of his truck, kicks his beater, gets back in, revs his engine, and speeds off into the horizon.
Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.
Whiskers swipes at your hand when you try to pet her. No claws. But the message is clear.
You wake up to scratching noises. Something is ON your car.
You look up. A family of raccoons is making themselves at home on your roof.
Mom raccoon. Dad raccoon. Three baby raccoons. All staring at you through the windshield with those little bandit masks.
You honk the horn. They don't flinch. You honk again. The babies yawn.
You stare at each other for a full minute. They're not leaving. This is their car now.
You go back to sleep. In the morning, they've left a dead mouse on your hood. A gift? A threat? Both?
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
